claim: Context engineering is a discipline that systematically provides models with the right information, tools, and instructions at the correct format and time to accomplish a specific task, shifting focus from prompt engineering to building dynamic systems that assemble structured context.
actionable: Design your AI systems with explicit context assembly pipelines rather than relying on clever single prompts; audit what context each LLM invocation receives and when.

claim: 95% of AI projects or AI pilot projects are failing according to a 2025 MIT study, primarily because agents lack proper context and coordination when operating in swarms without structure and orchestration.
actionable: Treat context engineering and agent orchestration as critical success factors; don't deploy swarms of agents without explicit coordination mechanisms and context management.

claim: Knowledge graphs represent data in both human-readable and machine-readable formats through nodes (entities) and edges (relationships), making connections explicit unlike vector-based RAG which obscures relationships in numerical embeddings.
actionable: For systems requiring transparent reasoning and relationship traversal, use knowledge graphs instead of pure vector search; combine both approaches for hybrid search when you need both semantic similarity and relational clarity.

claim: Context poisoning occurs in naive RAG systems when retrieval returns off-target information that's adjacent to but not what you actually need, leading to agents receiving misleading context.
actionable: When implementing RAG, validate retrieved chunks aren't just semantically similar but actually relevant to the query; use knowledge graphs or hybrid search to filter out adjacent but irrelevant information.

claim: Context window placement matters: put the most important context at the top of your prompt because models forget information placed at the bottom, as Anthropic's guidelines recommend.
actionable: Reorganize your prompts to place critical constraints, examples, and context at the beginning, not the end; test context ordering as a tuning parameter.

claim: Providing too much context to an agent can result in context poisoning or overloading; the key is providing the right information in the right structure at the right time, not maximum information.
actionable: Implement context filtering and prioritization mechanisms; don't assume more context is better; test with reduced context sets and measure quality metrics.

claim: Memory management in agents involves deciding what information agents should forget (typically older context in favor of newer), which is analogous to managing a forgetful character like Dory in Finding Nemo.
actionable: Implement explicit memory management strategies where agents deprioritize older conversation context; test whether your system should forget older messages, older sessions, or both.

claim: Tools and function calling (represented as MacGyver with the right tools) allow agents to delegate heavy lifting to specialized tools rather than doing computation themselves, letting them focus on reasoning.
actionable: Design tool interfaces that handle computation and I/O; train your agent to recognize when to call tools instead of trying to compute answers directly.

claim: Knowledge graphs can answer not just 'what is relevant to this query' but 'what else is connected to this topic that's necessary to include', enabling traversal for comprehensive context.
actionable: Use graph traversal queries (like variable-path Cypher queries) to automatically expand context from a seed node to related entities and relationships, not just semantic similarity matching.

claim: Context graphs (decision traces) allow agents to explain why they made a decision by storing and traversing the history of reasoning, making agent decisions auditable in high-stakes domains like credit decisions.
actionable: For regulated or high-consequence decisions, implement context graphs that record the agent's reasoning path; make this explainability a first-class feature, not an afterthought.

claim: Text-to-Cypher tools convert natural language queries into graph database query language, allowing non-technical users to interact with knowledge graphs by abstracting away the syntax details.
actionable: Implement text-to-Cypher retrieval for your knowledge graph; this lets agents and users query structured data via natural language without learning graph query syntax.
